title: Dog Taking Short Steps with Back Legs: Causes & Triage Guide
meta_desc: Is your dog taking short steps with their back legs? Discover potential causes like IVDD or arthritis and learn when you need to see a vet.
content_html:
Understanding Short Strides in the Hind Legs

When a dog takes short, choppy, or stilted steps with their back legs, it is typically a sign that they are attempting to limit the range of motion in their hips, knees, or spine to avoid discomfort. This "stilted gait" is a common compensatory behavior seen in dogs dealing with orthopedic or neurological issues.

Is it an Emergency?

The urgency level for this symptom is Medium. While short steps are rarely an immediate life-threatening crisis, they do indicate significant pain or a developing condition that requires veterinary attention within 24 to 48 hours. If your dog also loses the ability to walk, drags their paws, or exhibits a "drunk" or wobbly walk, you should seek emergency care immediately.

Why a Video or Photo Helps Triage

Capturing a video of your dog walking is one of the most helpful things you can do for a veterinarian. Dogs often experience an adrenaline surge at the clinic that can temporarily mask pain or cause them to walk differently than they do at home. A video of the gait in their natural environment, along with photos of their standing posture, allows the vet to see the true nature of the movement and pinpoint whether the issue is coming from the spine, hips, or lower joints.

Potential Causes for a Shortened Gait

Common causes include hip dysplasia, arthritis, Intervertebral Disc Disease (IVDD), or a cranial cruciate ligament (CCL) injury. A vet will use your visual evidence along with a physical exam to determine if the issue is structural or neurological.

faqs.question: What should I do at home while waiting for the vet appointment?
faqs.answer: Restrict your dog's activity immediately. Avoid all jumping, stair climbing, or rough play. Keep them on a leash for short bathroom breaks only to prevent worsening a potential spinal or joint injury.

faqs.question: Can I give my dog over-the-counter human pain relief?
faqs.answer: No. Many human medications like ibuprofen or naproxen are highly toxic to dogs. Even aspirin can cause stomach ulcers and may prevent your vet from prescribing better, safer medications during your visit.

faqs.question: Could this just be signs of old age?
faqs.answer: While older dogs often develop arthritis, taking short steps is a sign of pain, not just 'slowing down.' Most age-related gait changes can be significantly improved with the right medical management and environmental support.
